coredata.dc:title: Peptide-modified carbon dot aggregates for ultrasensitive detection of lipopolysaccharide through aggregation-induced emission enhancement
coredata.dc:creator: Madhu, Manivannan
Krishna Kumar, A. Santhana
Lu, Chi-Yu
Tseng, Wei-Lung
coredata.dc:description: This study fabricated yellow-emitting CDs (Y-CDs) by hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and urea and applied them as a fluorescence turn-on platform for sensitive and selective detection of lipopolysaccharide (LPS) based on the non-shifted AIEE of peptide-stabilized CD aggregates. The designed peptide (named K3) consisting of aggregation-active and LPS-recognition units triggered the aggregation of Y-CDs, switching on their fluorescence through the blue-shifted AIEE process. The formed K3-stabilized Y-CD aggregates (K3-YCDAs) specifically interacted with LPS at neutral pH, demonstrating that the sequence of the decorated peptide was highly connected with their selectivity and sensitivity. The K3-YCDAs provided a fast response time (within 5 min) to detect LPS with a quantification range of 0.5–100.0 nM and a limit of detection (LOD, signal-to-noise ratio of 3) of 300.0 pM. By integrating ultrafiltration membranes as a concentration device with K3-YCDAs as a sensing probe, the LOD for LPS was further reduced to 3.0 pM. The determination of picomolar levels of plasma LPS by the K3-YCDAs coupled to the centrifugation ultrafiltration was demonstrated to fall within the specificity range of clinical interest for sepsis patients. Also, the K3-YCDAs served as a fluorescent probe to selectively image and quantify E. coli cells. The distinct advantages of the K3-YCDAs for LPS include fast response time, wide linear range, low detection limit, and excellent selectivity compared to previously reported sensors.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The morphology and optical properties of the Y-CDs and K3-YCDAs. (A, B) TEM images of the (A) Y-CDs and (B) K3-YCDAs. The red cycle indicates the formation of K3-YCDAs. (C) DLS spectra of the Y-CDs and K3-YCDAs. (D, E) FTIR spectra of the (D) Y-CDs and (E) K3-YCDAs. (F) Fluorescence spectra of the Y-CDs and K3-YCDAs. The Y-CDs and K3-YCDAs were present in 30 mM phosphate buffer (pH 7.0). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Sensing of LPS by the K3-YCDAs. (A) Schematic illustration of the mechanism associated with the sensing of the K3-YCDAs toward LPS. (i) The K3 peptides induce the blue-shifted AIEE of the Y-CDs, while LPS trigger the non-shifted AIEE. (B) Fluorescence spectra and photographs of the K3-YCDAs (a) before and (b) after incubating with 100 nM LPS in 30 mM phosphate buffer (pH 7.0). (C) TEM image and (D) DLS spectra obtained from a solution of the K3-YCDAs and LPS. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Sensing of LPS by the K3-YCDAs. (A) Time-course fluorescence intensity response at 565 nm of the K3-YCDAs in the presence of 100 nM LPS. (B) Fluorescence spectra response of the K3-YCDAs after the addition of 0–100 nM LPS. (C) A plot of fluorescence intensity (565 nm) of the K3-YCDAs versus the concentration of LPS. Insets: nonlinear and linear fitting curves for quantifying 0.5–10 nM and 10–100 nM LPS, respectively. (D) Fluorescence intensity response at 565 nm of the K3-YCDAs incubating with LPS (100 nM) and other interfering compounds (100 nM) in 30 mM phosphate buffer (pH 7.0). Analytes: (a) no analyte, (b) heparin, (c) hyaluronic acid, (d) glutathione, (e) adenosine monophosphate, (f) adenosine diphosphate, (g) adenosine triphosphate, (h) histidine, (i) bovine serum albumin, (j) glucose, and (k) LPS. Error bars represent standard deviations from three measurements. The dash line indicates the fluorescence intensity of the K3-YCDAs without the addition of analytes.

Ultrasenstive detection of LPS based on the combination of the K3-YCDAs and ultrafiltration membrane. (A) Schematic illustration of the K3-YCDAs for the sensing of concentrated LPS from ultrafiltration membrane. (B) fluorescence spectra response obtained from the extraction of 5–750 pM LPS with the ultrafiltration membrane, followed by the incubation of the K3-YCDAs. (C) A plot of the fluorescence intensity (560 nm) of the K3-YCDAs versus the concentration of the extracted LPS. A nonlinear fitting curve for quantifying 5–750 pM LPS. The sensing experiment was operated in 30 mM phosphate buffer (pH 7.0). (B, C) Error bars represent standard deviations from three measurements.

Sensing of LPS in real-world samples. (A) Fluorescence spectra response measured from the pretreatment of LPS (25 pM)-spiked human plasma with the ultrafiltration membrane, followed by the incubation of the K3-YCADs. Inset: Histograms of the average fluorescence intensity (565 nm) of the K3-YCDAs upon the addition of the spiked LPS at concentration of 0 and 25 pM. (B) CLSM, bright-field, and merge images obtained from the labelling of E. coli and S. aureus with the K3-YCDAs. (C) Quantification of E. coli cells by the K3-YCDAs. (D) A plot of the fluorescence intensity (565 nm) of the K3-YCDAs versus the concentration of E. coli cells. A nonlinear fitting curve for quantifying LPS. Inset: UV-excited photographs of the K3-YCDAs (a) before and (b) after the addition of 4.0 × 107 cells mL−1 E. coli. (A, D) Error bars represent standard deviations from three measurements.
Comparison of the previously reported sensors for sensing LPS.

head.ce:title: Peptide-modified carbon dot aggregates for ultrasensitive detection of lipopolysaccharide through aggregation-induced emission enhancement

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study fabricated yellow-emitting CDs (Y-CDs) by hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and urea and applied them as a fluorescence turn-on platform for sensitive and selective detection of lipopolysaccharide (LPS) based on the non-shifted AIEE of peptide-stabilized CD aggregates. The designed peptide (named K3) consisting of aggregation-active and LPS-recognition units triggered the aggregation of Y-CDs, switching on their fluorescence through the blue-shifted AIEE process. The formed K3-stabilized Y-CD aggregates (K3-YCDAs) specifically interacted with LPS at neutral pH, demonstrating that the sequence of the decorated peptide was highly connected with their selectivity and sensitivity. The K3-YCDAs provided a fast response time (within 5 min) to detect LPS with a quantification range of 0.5–100.0 nM and a limit of detection (LOD, signal-to-noise ratio of 3) of 300.0 pM. By integrating ultrafiltration membranes as a concentration device with K3-YCDAs as a sensing probe, the LOD for LPS was further reduced to 3.0 pM. The determination of picomolar levels of plasma LPS by the K3-YCDAs coupled to the centrifugation ultrafiltration was demonstrated to fall within the specificity range of clinical interest for sepsis patients. Also, the K3-YCDAs served as a fluorescent probe to selectively image and quantify E. coli cells. The distinct advantages of the K3-YCDAs for LPS include fast response time, wide linear range, low detection limit, and excellent selectivity compared to previously reported sensors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Lipopolysaccharide (LPS), consisting of lipid A, core oligosaccharide, and O-antigen polysaccharide chain, is a prominent component of Gram-negative bacterial outer membrane. Treating bacterial cells with membrane-targeting antibiotics triggers the release of massive LPS, inducing an inflammatory response through the recognition of Toll-like receptors on cells [1]. This event leads to serious medical problems, including septic shock, severe sepsis, hypotension, organ dysfunction, and possibly death. Even a minimal amount of LPS (1.0 ng) in the bloodstream can cause shivering and fever [2,3]. The average plasma level of LPS in patients with sepsis was reported to be 581 ± 49 pg mL−1, which is remarkably higher than the control value (5.1 ± 7.3 pg mL−1) in Sepsis-free subjects [4]. Due to its acute toxicity, developing a sensitive method for monitoring LPS levels in biological samples is indispensable. Gel-clot Limulus amebocyte lysate (LAL) test is the standard approach for qualitative analysis of LPS based on the LPS-stimulated coagulation of Limulus polyphemus blood involving multiple enzyme reactions. Following the same methodology, the investigators introduced chromogenic and kinetic turbidimetric LAL assays to quantify LPS through monitoring the development of yellow color intensity and the turbidity of the reaction, respectively [5]. Although the LAL-related assay provides satisfactory sensitivity (detection limit, 1.0 pg mL−1) toward LPS, its detection result is easily interfered with by environmental change (e.g., pH and temperature), proteases, and carbohydrate derivatives.

In response to these limitations, numerous methods have been proposed to detect LPS in real-world samples, including gas chromatography coupled with mass spectrometry [6], enzyme-linked immune sorbent assay [5], capillary electrophoresis with laser-induced fluorescence [7], electrochemical platforms [8], and chromophore sensors [9]. Recently, LPS-responsive fluorescent probes have received considerable interest due to their high sensitivity, fast response time, miniaturization, and portability (Table 1 ) [10–14]. The previously reported LPS-responsive probes can be classified into three groups, including fluorescent polymers, small organic fluorophores, and luminescent nanomaterials. Modification of the above-mentioned materials with cationic proteins, cationic peptides, aptamers, antibodies, and cells allows them to recognize negatively charged polysaccharide- and hydrophobic lipid-containing LPS through electrostatic attraction and hydrophobic interaction [15]. Examples of earlier published sensors include 3-phenylthiophene-based water-soluble co-polythiophenes [9], tetraphenylethylene- and imidazolium-modified alkane molecules [16], peptide-functionalized polydiacetyl liposomes [17], ROX-labeled aptamer-adsorbed graphene oxides [18], fluorophore-labeled peptide-assembled graphene oxides [19], Rhodamine-conjugated peptide-attached WS2–Pt–Fe2O3 polycaprolactone Janus micromotors [20], and polylysine-conjugated carbon dots (CDs) [21]. However, controllable preparation of fluorescent polymers and organic molecules and other probes is quite sophisticated to tune their optical properties and recognition ability [22–24]. In addition, in most of the above probes, modification of recognition elements with fluorophores may hinder their selectivity for LPS. Therefore, there is a great need to develop a label-free and easy-to-fabricate probe that will improve the selectivity for LPS and reduce the cost of preparation. More importantly, no previous studies have shown that the fluorescent probes can provide satisfactory sensitivity to differentiate the plasma LPS levels in normal subjects from those with sepsis.

Carbon dots (CDs) have been massively studied in the fields of light-emitting devices [25,26], biosensors [27,28], bioimaging [29], and anti-counterfeiting [30,31] owing to their unique luminescence features, such as excitation-dependent emission, good photobleaching resistance [32], and size- and surface defect-tunable emission [33]. Nevertheless, most CDs experience the aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching, hindering their applications in fabricating fluorescence turn-on sensors through analyte-induced aggregation [34]. To overcome this limitation, recent efforts have been devoted to the developments of CDs featuring aggregation-induced emission (AIE) and aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) [35]. The AIE- and AIEE-related CDs have been demonstrated to be well-suited for fluorescence turn-on sensing of metal ions [34,36,37], triphosphate adenosine [38], formaldehyde [39], and chlortetracycline [40]. To data, there is a lack of previous studies reporting on the sensing of LPS by AIEE-related CDs. Thus, we herein fabricated peptide-stabilized, yellow-emitting CD (Y-CD) aggregates to sense LPS in real-world samples via the non-shifted AIEE process. The key motivation for selecting peptide as a recognition element is the fact that it can include AIEE-active sequence (lysine repeats) and LPS-recognition sequence. The preparation of the peptide-stabilized Y-CD aggregates could be accomplished by directly mixing the Y-CDs and the designed peptide under the optimal pH condition. In contrast to the Y-CDs, the quantum yield (QY) and of fluorescence lifetime of the formed Y-CD aggregates were improved to be 5.27% and 4.39 ns, respectively, owing to the blue-shifted AIEE process. The presence of LPS triggered the assembly of the formed Y-CD aggregates through non-shifted AIEE process, switching on their fluorescence. The peptide sequence was found to be a determining factor in affecting the selectivity of the Y-CD aggregates toward LPS. These observations could open the opportunity to fabricate a new class of AIEE-active nanoprobes through the interplay among the other related Y-CDs, peptides, and target analytes.

Citric acid was purchased from JT Baker (Phillipsburg, NJ, USA). Urea, LPS (MW 10 kDa), bovine serum albumin (BSA), heparin sodium salt (from porcine intestinal mucosa, MW 12 kDa), glutathione (GSH), adenosine monophosphate, adenosine diphosphate, adenosine triphosphate, bovine hyaluronidase (from bovine tests), glucose, and histidine were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St Louis, MO, USA). Three lysine-rich peptides, including KNYSSSISSIHC (named K1; MW 1325), KKKNYSSSISSIHC (named K3, MW 1581), and KKKKKNYSSSISSIHC (named K5, MW 1838), were ordered from Synpeptide (Shanghai, China). Other analytical-grade reagents, buffers, solvents, and chemicals were listed in the Supporting Information.

Absorption, fluorescence, and Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a JASCO V-670 spectrophotometer (JASCO, Tokyo, Japan), a Hitachi F-7000 fluorometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan), and a PerkinElmer Spectrum 100 spectrometer (Waltham, MA, USA), respectively. Electron microscopy measurements were performed on transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEM-2100, JEOL, Japan) operating at 200 kV. The elemental composition and oxidation state were separately detected using TEM equipped with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS; X-Max 80, Oxford instruments; Abingdon, UK) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Quantera SXM, ULVAC-PHI, Inc., Japan) with a monochromatic Al Kα radiation (hν, 1486.6 eV). ζ-potentials and hydrodynamic diameters were recorded on a DelsaTM nano zeta potential (Beckman Coulter, USA) and a submicron particle size analyzer (Beckman Coulter Inc., USA), respectively. AFM images were measured on a scanning probe microscope (Dimension 3100, Veeco Digital Instruments, Santa Barbara, CA, USA).

The preparation of the Y-CDs was performed according to an earlier published method with slight modifications [41]. Briefly, a mixture of 1.0 g citric acid and 1.0 g urea was dissolved in 7.0 mL Milli-Q water. Subsequently, the resultant solution was transferred to a 20.0 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. The obtained solution was left at ambient temperature for two days to remove large-sized products by gravity sedimentation. After that, the resulting reddish-brown solution was centrifuged at 12 000 rpm for 20 min. The Y-CDs in the supernatant were further purified two times by a 0.22 μm PVDF membrane. The mass concentration of the purified Y-CDs was determined to be 50.0 mg mL−1 by weighting the difference between the dried Y-CD-containing tube and the empty tube.

Three kinds of peptide-stabilized Y-CD aggregates were separately prepared by mixing the Y-CDs (200 μL, 65.0 mg mL−1) and peptides (including K1, K3, and K5; 70.0 μL, 0.1 mg mL−1) in 30 mM phosphate buffer (pH 3.0–10.0). The resulting solutions were incubated at ambient temperature for 15 min. Afterward, different concentrations of LPS (200.0 μL, 0.02–40 nM) reacted with peptide-stabilized Y-CD aggregates (200.0 μL, 55.0 mg mL−1) in 30 mM phosphate buffer (pH 7.0), followed by incubating at ambient temperature for 0–30 min. The fluorescence spectra of the resultant solutions were measured at an excitation wavelength of 510 nm. The selectivity of the present probes was evaluated by replacing LPS with other possible interfering compounds, one at a time. For the sake of lowering the detection limit of LPS, 500.0 μL of 5.0–750.0 pM LPS were enriched by centrifugation ultrafiltration (molecular weight cut-off of 3 kDa). The obtained extracts (200.0 μL) were incubated with peptide-stabilized Y-CD aggregates (200.0 μL, ∼16.0 mg mL−1) in 30 mM phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) for 5 min. The following steps are the same as the procedure associated with the sensing of LPS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of LPS in human plasma
ce:section.ce:para: The following procedures and protocols, approved by the Institutional Review Board of the Human Research Ethics Committee of National Cheng University (NCKU HREC-F-107-044-2), conformed to the ethical guidelines under the Declaration of Helsinki. Human blood samples collected from a healthy adult male, the age of 26 years, were immediately centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 30 min at 4 °C according to the previously reported method [42]. A portion (50.0 μL) of the obtained plasma samples spiked with 1.0 and 25.0 pM LPS and was diluted to 10-fold with 30 mM phosphate buffer (pH 7.0). The spiked samples (500.0 μL) were concentrated by centrifugation ultrafiltration (molecular weight cut-off of 3 kDa). The obtained extracts (200.0 μL) were mixed with the K3 peptide-stabilized Y-CD aggregates (K3-YCDAs; 200 μL, ∼16 mg mL−1) in 30 mM phosphate buffer (pH 7.0). After 5 min, the fluorescence spectra of the resultant solution were measured at an excitation wavelength of 510 nm.

Escherichia coli (E. Coli O157:H7) and Streptococcus aureus (BCRC 15211; S. aureus) were bought from the Bioresource Collection and Research Center in Taiwan. Samples of S. aureus and E. Coli were cultured in a sterilized Luria-Bertani medium (25.0 g L−1; Difco, Becton Dickinson, Sparks, Md.) for around 14–16 h at 37 °C until their optical density (OD) at 600 nm in liquid culture reached approximately 1.0. Under such conditions, the concentration of bacteria is approximately 5.0 × 108–1.0 × 109 cells mL−1. The bacterial cells were labeled with the K3–Y-CDAs and imaged by CLSM equipped with a 64 × objective and 488 nm laser. The fluorescence of the K3–Y-CDAGs in the bacterial cells was collected in an orange channel (λem = 530–600 nm). Moreover, E. coli cells were diluted with 30 mM phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) before measurement. Different concentrations of E. coli cells (200.0 μL, 2.5 × 106–4.0 × 107 cells mL−1) were incubated with the K3-YCDAs (200.0 μL, 16.0 mg mL−1) in 30 mM phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) for 5 min, followed by recording fluorescence spectra of the resultant solution at an excitation wavelength of 510 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peptide-induced blue-shifted AIEE of the Y-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The Y-CDs were hydrothermally prepared using citric acid as a carbon source [27] and urea as a quantum-yield enhancer [43,44]. The detailed procedure associated with the hydrothermal treatment and purification was illustrated in the Supporting Information (Fig. S1). The loading of specific ligands (e.g., peptides and aptamers) to the surface of the Y-CDs allows the recognition of target analytes. Considering that the surface zeta potential of the Y-CDs was measured to be −27.0 mV in 10.0 mM phosphate buffer (pH 7.0), we propose that the ligands with cationic end groups could electrostatically interact with the Y-CDs, thus empowering their surface with the ability to recognize the target. In confirmation of this hypothesis, the peptide (amino sequence, KKKNYSSSISSIHC; named K3; pI = 9.6) containing the LPS recognition sequence (KNYSSSISSIHC) and two lysine repeats was rationally designed to electrostatically bind to the Y-CDs in 30.0 mM phosphate buffer (pH 7.0). The LPS recognition part of the K3 peptide has been demonstrated to specifically bind to LPS with a dissociation constant of 0.01 nM [45–47]. Moreover, the lysine repeat part of the K3 peptide exhibits positively charged amine side chains, allowing the electrostatic attraction with the Y-CDs. After incubating the Y-CDs with the K3 peptide, the morphology and chemical composition of the resultant products were characterized by electron microscopy- and spectroscopy-related techniques and their characteristics were compared with the Y-CDs. The TEM images indicate that the average core size of the Y-CDs and resultant products were 2.04 ± 0.45 and 18.2 ± 7.9 nm, respectively (Fig. 1 A, B). The dynamic light scattering (DLS) was also implemented to measure the difference in hydrodynamic diameter between the Y-CDs and resultant products. Fig. 1C shows that the mixing of the K3 peptide and Y-CDs induced a change in the hydrodynamic diameter from 4.1 ± 2.9 to 17.2 ± 10.8 nm. These observations indicate that the K3 peptide triggers the aggregation of the Y-CDs, forming the K3-YCDAs. Additionally, the surface zeta potentials of the Y-CDs and K3-YCDAs were separately measured to be −27.0 and −14.0 mV. Evidently, the positively charged K3 peptide shields the surface charges of the synthesized Y-CDs, lowering the negative surface charges of the Y-CDs [48]. The subsequent study exploited the Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy to examine the functional groups of the Y-CDs and K3-YCDAs (Fig. 1D and E). The Y-CDs exhibited a similar FT-IR spectrum to the K3-YCDAs, except that the K3-YCDAs possess an additional FT-IR peak at 1550 cm−1. The peak at 1550 cm−1 was suggested to originate from amide C–N/N–H stretching bands of the K3 peptide [49,50]. This result reflects that the K3-YCDAs is indeed composed of the Y-CDs and the K3 peptide. The Y-CDs’ XPS survey spectrum appeared three peaks at 284.0, 399.0, and 531.0 eV corresponding to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, in sequence (Fig. S2A). The Y-CD's deconvoluted C 1s and N 1s peaks reveal the prominent peaks at 283.5 and 398.6 eV, respectively, identified as C–C/CC and C–N–C bonds (Fig. S2B, S2C). The resolved O 1s peaks of the Y-CDs offer two components at 531.4 and 530.6 eV, which are separately attributable to CO and C–OH/C–O–C bonds (Fig. S2D). The high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s in the K3-YCDAs were almost the same as those in the Y-CDs (Fig. S3), signifying that the K3-mediated assembly of the Y-CDs rarely alters their backbone structure. It is concluded that the Y-CDs and K3-YCDAs contain π-conjugated carbon and pyridinic nitrogen with hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups.

We next compared the optical properties of the Y-CDs with the K3-YCDAs. Under an excitation wavelength of 510 nm, the fluorescence peaks of the Y-CDs and K3-YCDAs appeared at 580 and 565 nm, respectively. In contrast to the Y-CDs, the quantum yield (QY; Φ f ) and fluorescence lifetime (τ) of the K3-YCDAs were improved from 3.42 to 5.27% and 3.1–4.39 ns, respectively. The following discussion can interpret this enhanced fluorescence intensity and lifetime. The interacted K3 peptides in the aggregates could restrict the rotation and vibration of the Y-CD's function groups (i.e., hydroxyl and carboxyl groups) and reduce the non-radiative decay process, enhancing the QY value and long fluorescence lifetime according to the following equations:
(1)

nr correspond to radiative and non-radiative rates, respectively. Also, the reduced solvation of the hydrophilic functional groups lowers the non-radiative decay of the Y-CDs [51], facilitating the improvement of QY and prolongation of fluorescence lifetime. Likewise, previous studies have demonstrated that most AIEE-related CD aggregates, compared to the CDs without stimulators, exhibit better QY and increased fluorescence lifetime (Table 2 ). Besides, the K3 peptide-induced blueshift in the emission of Y-CDs could result from the change in their microenvironment from water (dispersed state) to a nonpolar region (i.e., aggregated state) [52,53]. Notably, an increase in the solvent polarity lowers the excited state energy level since the solvent relaxation time (10−10 s) is faster than the vibration relaxation time (10−12 s). In short, the K3-stimulated aggregation of Y-CD underwent the blue-shifted AIEE process due to the particle aggregation and solvent polarity effect. It is worth mentioning that the modification of K3 not only enhances the brightness of the Y-CDs but also endows them with the ability to sense LPS molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: LPS-triggered non-shifted AIEE of the K3-YCDAs
ce:section.ce:para: Given that LPS consists of negatively charged saccharide units and lipid tails, we rule out the electrostatic attraction between the K3-YCDAs (zeta potential = −14.0 mV) and LPS. It is proposed that the abundant hydroxyl side chains of serine units in the designed peptide could cause hydrogen bonding with saccharide units of LPS [19]. Besides, the hydrophobic side chain of tyrosine, histidine, and isoleucine units in the designed peptide is suggested to interact with the lipid tail of LPS. According to these two assumptions, the added LPS could induce the assembly of the K3-YCDAs, switching on their fluorescence via the AIEE process (Fig. 2 A). In addition to verify this assumption, the stability of the K3-YCDAs was explored at 25֯ C under different pH values with varying time periods (Fig. S4). It is evident that the fluorescence of K3-YCDAs remained almost constant in the pH range of 3.0–10.0 and at pH 7.0 within 30 min. We next compared the fluorescence spectra of the K3-YCDAs in the absence and presence of LPS. After incubating the K3-YCDAs with 100 nM LPS in 30 mM phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) for 30 min, the resulting products showed approximately 2-fold higher fluorescence intensity than K3-YCDAs (Fig. 2B). In contrast to the K3-YCDAs, the resulting products fluoresced bright orange under UV light irradiation (inset in Fig. 2B). We measured their TEM image and DLS spectrum to clarify whether the resulting products' enhanced fluorescence originates from the LPS-promoted aggregation of the K3-YCDAs. The TEM image reveals the formation of spherical-like aggregates with different sizes in a solution of LPS and the K3-YCDAs (Fig. 2C). Besides, the DLS spectrum displays that the resulting product has a large size distribution with a hydrodynamic diameter of about 500 nm (Fig. 2D). Evidently, the resulting products are larger than the K3-YCDAs in terms of particle size and hydrodynamic diameter, supporting that LPS triggers the aggregation of the K3-YCDAs. Furthermore, the net surface charge of the aggregated K3-YCDAs, measured by a zeta potential analyzer, approached nearly zero due to the severe nanoparticle aggregation. This observation is in line with the results obtained from the TEM and DLS analyses. However, the maximum fluorescence wavelength of the obtained products was almost the same as that of the K3-YCDAs. This finding is inconsistent with the K3-mediated AIEE of the Y-CDs, which induces a blue shift in maximum fluorescence wavelength. This phenomenon could reflect that the solvent polarity effect rarely affects the maximum emission wavelength of the K3-YCDAs, even in their aggregated state.

We next turned our interest to the effect of the peptide sequence on the detection capability of peptide-stability Y-CD aggregates. Two peptides with the sequences of KNYSSSISSIHC (named K1) and KKKKKNYSSSISSIHC (named K5) were used to trigger the aggregation of the Y-CDs, resulting in the formation of the K1-YCDAs and K5-YCDAs, correspondingly. Fig. S5 shows the TEM images of K1- and K5-YCDAs, confirming that the K1 and K5 peptides also induced the aggregation of the Y-CDs. Compared to the K3 peptide, the K1 peptide caused a relatively low aggregation degree of the Y-CDs. Moreover, the size of the Y-CD aggregates induced by the K5 peptide is much larger than that triggered by the K3 peptide. These findings suggest that more lysine repeats in the designed peptide have stronger interaction with the Y-CDs, promoting their higher aggregation. Fig. S6A displays that the presence of 100 nM LPS caused the fluorescence quenching of the K1-YCDAs at pH 7.0. This phenomenon can be attributed to the LPS-mediated removal of K1 peptide from K1-YCDAs. In other word, the K1 peptide interacts weakly with the Y-CDs since it contains only the peptide sequence of LPS recognition. In contrast to the K3-YCDAs, the fluorescence of the K5-YCDAs was less enhanced upon the addition of 100 nM LPS at pH 7.0 (Fig. S6B). It is suggested that the large-sized K5-YCDAs have higher steric hindrance for interacting LPS than the small-sized K3-YCDAs. These findings strongly demonstrate that the K3-YCDAs are superior to the K1- and K5-YCDAs in their response to LPS molecules.

Before conducting the quantification-related experiments, we monitored the time-course response of the K3-YCDAs in the presence of LPS. Fig. 3 A reveals that the reaction time of the K3-YCDAs with LPS was optimized to be 5 min in 30 mM phosphate buffer (pH 7.0). As the concentration of LPS varied from 0 to 100.0 nM, the fluorescence of the K3-YCDAs was progressively enhanced (Fig. 3B). Plotting the fluorescence intensity (565 nm) of the K3-YCDAs against the concentration of LPS generated the linear and nonlinear calibration curves over the range of 10.0–100.0 nM (R 2 = 0.9957; y = 15.34x + 3159) and 0.5–10.0 nM (R 2 = 0.9782; y = 2910x + 0.0569), respectively (Fig. 3C). The fluorescence peak intensity's relative standard deviations (RSD) were less than 3%, suggesting that the K3-YCDAs can detect a slight change in the LPS level. The average intra‐assay (n = 5) and inter‐assay (n = 5 per day for 3 days) RSD values from quantifying three different concentrations (1, 10, and 100 nM) of LPS by the K3-YCDAs were determined to be 4.5 and 6.5%, respectively. The limit of detection (LOD; signal-to-noise ratio of 3) of LPS was determined to be 300.0 pM (approximately 3.0 ng/mL), which is superior to that detected by most of the previously reported sensors (Table 1) [10–14,17,19,21,54–69]. Moreover, compared to the earlier published sensors, the K3-YCDAs provide a relatively large linear range and a short time for quantifying LPS. Fig. 3D reveals that the K3-YCDAs exhibited excellent selectivity toward LPS. Heparin, adenosine triphosphate, adenosine diphosphate, and adenosine monophosphate caused a negligible increase in the fluorescence of the K3-YCDAs, confirming that negatively charged polysaccharides (polysaccharide found in the LPS) and phosphate-containing molecules (functional groups found in the LPS) barely interact with the K3-YCDAs. The numerous hydroxyl groups (functional groups found in the LPS) of hyaluronic acid and glucose may form hydrogen bonding with serine units of the attached K3 peptide at neutral pH, but its strength is too weak to trigger the assembly of the K3-YCDAs. The other molecules (glutathione, histidine, and bovine serum albumin) commonly found in biological systems have the same fluorescence response upon the addition of the K3-YCDAs. The findings mentioned above clearly reflect that the K3 peptide specifically interacted with LPS [46,47]. In confirmation of the importance of the Y-CDs’ surface charges in detecting LPS, positively charged CDs (named PC-CDs) were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of benzene-1,2-diamine and l-histidine. The zeta potential of the as-made PC-CDs was measured to be +17.0 mV, demonstrating their cationic nature. The fluorescence peak of the PC-CDs stayed at 630 nm when varying the excitation wavelength from 460 to 620 nm (Fig. S7A). After simple mixing of different K3 concentrations and the PC-CDs, the resultant products showed a negligible enhancement in the fluorescence peak (Fig. S7B). This observation signifies the electrostatic repulsion between the K3 peptide and PC-CDs. Therefore, the PC-CDs without decorating the K3 peptide were incapable of detecting LPS via the non-shifted AIEE process (Fig. S7C).

Although the K3-YCDAs provide satisfactory sensitivity and selectivity toward LPS, their detection sensitivity is still insufficient to detect LPS in sepsis patients (581 ± 49 pg mL−1). To overcome this limitation, we propose to combine an ultrafiltration membrane with our proposed sensor to detect ultra-trace LPS molecules (Fig. 4 A). Previous studies have demonstrated that an ultrafiltration membrane can be used for enriching low concentration of proteins via the difference between the pore size of the membrane and the hydrodynamic diameter of proteins [70,71]. Since the molecular weight of LPS (approximately 10 kDa) is larger than the pore size of 3-kDa molecular weight cut-off ultrafiltration membrane, we reason that LPS molecules could be captured and enriched with the proposed ultrafiltration membrane. After a solution of 5.0–750.0 pM LPS was enriched by the proposed ultrafiltration membrane, the obtained extracts led to a progressive increase in the fluorescence intensity of the K3-YCDAs (Fig. 4B). A nonlinear calibration curve (R 2 = 0.9828; y = 1840x +0.0470) between the fluorescence intensity (565 nm) of the K3-YCDAs versus the concentration of LPS was observed in the range of 5.0–750.0 pM (Fig. 4C). This combined system provided the LODs corresponding to 3 pM (approximately 30 pg mL−1), which fall within the specificities of clinical interest for LPS. Evidently, the detection sensitivity of LPS can be improved to approximately 100-fold by integrating the ultrafiltration method with the K3-YCDAs.

Encouraged by the excellent sensing performance of the K3-YCDAs toward LPS, we attempted to explore whether the present probe integrated with the ultrafiltration membrane could be implemented to determine LPS in real-world samples. Samples of human plasma spiked with 25.0 pM LPS (approximately 250.0 pg mL−1) were pretreated with centrifugation ultrafiltration, followed by the sensing of the extracts with the K3-YCDAs. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence of the K3-YCDAs significantly increased with varying the spiked concentration of LPS from 0 to 25.0 pM in human plasma. The significant difference in the mean fluorescence intensity of the K3-YCDAs between 0 and 25.0 pM LPS in human plasma was observed in the inset of Fig. 5A. It is suggested that this integrated method could have great potential to identify sepsis patients in clinical samples. Considering that E. coli cells belonging to a Gram-negative bacterium contain LPS on their outer membrane [72], we assumed that the K3-YCDAs could be implemented for imaging E. coli through the specific binding of the K3 peptide to LPS. Gram-positive S. aureus (without LPS) was used as a control for this study. After E. coli and S. aureus were separately incubated with the K3-YCDAs and then imaged by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM), apparent fluorescence was only visualized in E. coli (Fig. 5B). This result demonstrates that the K3-YCDAs selectively detect E. coli, implying that the proposed probe can differentiate between Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. The selective fluorescence imaging of E. coli cells by the K3-YCDAs was exploited to quantify the E. coli level in an aqueous solution. A progressive increase in the fluorescence of the K3-YCDAs appeared with increasing the E. coli concentration from 0 to 2.5 × 108 cells mL−1 (Fig. 5C). The K3-YCDAs displayed a nonlinear response (R 2 = 0.9518; y = 2930x +0.0294) to the E. coli concentration from 5 × 106 to 2.5 × 108 cells mL−1 (Fig. 5D). Also, the K3-YCDAs allowed the naked-eye detection of E. coli at the concentration of 4.0 × 107 cells mL−1 under the irradiation of a UV lamp (inset in Fig. 5D). Consequently, the K3-YCDAs are expected to serve as a fluorescence turn-on probe to probe Gram-negative bacteria.

In summary, we have demonstrated that the peptide consisting of the LPS recognition sequence and repetitive lysine unit triggers the blue-shifted AIEE of the Y-CDs via electrostatic attraction. The formed K3-YCDAs are well-suited for fluorescence turn-on detection of LPS via the non-shifted AIEE process. In contrast to the previously reported sensors (Table 1), the K3-YCDAs manifest the advantages of fast response time (within 5 min), low detection limit (300.0 pM), wide quantification range (1.0–100.0 nM), excellent selectivity, and easy fabrication. The combination of the ultrafiltration membrane and our proposed sensor successfully detect 5.0–750.0 pM LPS in an aqueous solution and 25.0 pM LPS in human plasma. Additionally, the selective fluorescence imaging and quantification of E. coli cells are accomplished by specific interaction of the K3-YCDAs with the outer LPS layers. Given that peptides are intensively used as a recognition element to specifically interact with macromolecules, metal ions, and nucleic acids [42,48,73], we believe that the finding of this study will encourage investigators to design novel peptide-modified CD aggregates for sensing analytes of interest.
